answer:
**The Seafarer**, found in *Old English Elegies* (ch. 5), indeed pivots from the speaker's earthly hardships and sea-exile to a communal devotional exhortation, urging reflection on the eternal heavenly home.[^1] Its closing turn reads:

> Let us think where we have our home,  
> and then think how we may come thither,  
> and let us then strive that we may be allowed  
> into that eternal blessedness,  
> where life is born of the Lord's love,  
> our hope in the heavens. Thanks be to the Holy One,  
> the Prince of Glory, the everlasting Lord,  
> that He has honored us for all time.  
> Amen.[^1]

Cynewulf's poems in the corpus (*Andreas*, *Elene*, *Juliana*)—all attributed to him and featuring his characteristic runic signatures (typically woven into personal epilogues revealing his name)—likewise conclude with devotional pivots, though these vary by poem. *Andreas* (ch. 1) ends a heroic narrative with communal praise to God amid departure, less personally devotional:[^2]

> ...and then they honored the Owner of glory,  
> they cried in a chorus, and called out thus:  
> “One is the eternal God of all creation!  
> His might and His majesty over middle-earth  
> are famously blessed, and His glory over all  
> in heaven's high-throne shines on the holy,  
> beautiful in splendor for a broad age,  
> eternal with angels. He is the noble King!”[^2]

*Elene* (ch. 1) shifts to an extended eschatological homily on Judgment Day, fire's purification, and salvation for the repentant righteous (contrasting the damned), ending in doxology:[^3]

> So this whole world shall pass away...  
> Therefore they now shine in beauty,  
> like the angels, they enjoy the inheritance  
> of the Glory-King for all time. Amen.[^3]

*Juliana* (ch. 1) most closely mirrors the personal "runic epilogue" style (where Cynewulf signs his name in runes and breaks the narrative frame), pivoting to a direct plea for the audience's intercessory prayers on his behalf at Judgment:[^4]

> I ask every person of humankind,  
> who recites this poem, that he, mindful,  
> earnestly remember me by my name,  
> and pray to the Molder that the Helm of the heavens  
> grant me help...  
> Grant us, God of might,  
> that we may find Your face, joy of princes,  
> mild and merciful in that glorious time. Amen.[^4]

These closings perform overlapping theological work: each counters transience (exile, martyrdom, worldly history) with eternal orientation—homage to God's sovereignty, hope in heaven, and doxological *Amen*. The Seafarer's is a concise, urgent communal call to heavenly striving and gratitude, emphasizing divine honor amid mutability.

Cynewulf's, however, are richer overall. *Juliana*'s is intensely personal, inserting the poet (via rune-signature tradition) into soteriology, soliciting prayers for Judgment mercy from Father, Spirit, and Son (Trinity explicit). *Elene*'s doctrinal depth unfolds purgatorial refinement (righteous/sinful differentiated by fire's tiers, gold-purification metaphor), blending eschatology with assurance for penitents—more systematic than the Seafarer's broad "blessedness." *Andreas* aligns closest to the Seafarer's choral praise but lacks personal depth. Thus, while analogous in pivot, Cynewulf's integrate self-revelation, intercession, and nuanced afterlife theology, arguably "richer" in devotional intimacy and orthodoxy.[^5]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05-maey-ich-be-me-sylfum-soth-yied-wrekan-sithas-seya/translation/#p-1c332d).

[^2]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-be1571).

[^3]: Cynewulf, *Elene*, [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-dcbbfe).

[^4]: Cynewulf, *Juliana*, [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-1f8ce8).

[^5]: Nearest-corpus stats (e.g., *Juliana* ch.1 ↔ *Old English Elegies* ch.5 at 0.31 cosine) confirm stylistic kinship in devotional motifs.
